critic_raw:
This transformer vs. logistic regression post-discharge complication prediction experiment contains several important methodological problems.

1. PREPROCESSING FITTED ON ENTIRE DATASET BEFORE SPLITTING CONTRADICTS THE TEMPORAL SPLIT DESIGN. The design employs a temporal split (training: ~22 months, validation: ~7 months, test: ~7 months of recent data) — a methodologically sound choice. However, it simultaneously states: 'preprocessing transformers (tokenizer, text normalizer, vocabulary builder) are fit on the entire dataset before splitting.' This directly undermines the temporal split: the vocabulary and tokenization are informed by clinical notes written in the test period. For a transformer model, the vocabulary affects which tokens get assigned learned embeddings and which get split into subword units. Notes from the test period (most recent ~7 months) will have their term representations informed by patterns from future notes relative to the training window.

2. HYPERPARAMETER SEARCH WITH FINAL CROSS-VERIFICATION ON THE TEST SET INVALIDATES THE HOLDOUT. Section 6 states: 'Hyperparameter tuning is performed mostly using the validation set, but a final cross-verification on the test set is conducted to confirm the chosen hyperparameter configuration maintains its performance edge.' This is explicit test-set-based model selection. 'Confirming the configuration maintains its advantage' means rejecting configurations where test-set performance is lower than validation-set performance — a form of selection that incorporates test-set information into the final model choice. Any reported test-set metrics are not holdout estimates.

3. MULTI-CLASS COMPLICATION LABEL AGGREGATION LOSES CLINICALLY RELEVANT INFORMATION. The design assigns each patient 'the primary or most severe complication' as a single class label. Patients with multiple complications will have their secondary complications ignored, and the severity ordering will be determined by a clinical judgment call that is not pre-specified. This creates label noise for patients with multiple simultaneous complications, and the 'most severe' judgment may encode clinical knowledge that is correlated with the clinical note text (since the note was written by clinicians who made these severity judgments). This is a form of confounded label construction.

4. CLINICALBERT/BIOBERT END-TO-END FINE-TUNING WITH 100,000 RECORDS AND 3 YEARS OF DATA — CLASS BALANCE IS NOT SPECIFIED. The transformer is trained end-to-end on 60,000 training records. Without knowing the complication class distribution, the class-weighted loss function parameter space is unknown. If some complications are extremely rare (e.g., fewer than 500 training cases), the transformer will not see enough examples of that class to learn meaningful features, and macro-F1 will be dominated by performance on the few-shot classes. The design does not specify complication prevalence, making the macro-F1 claim impossible to evaluate a priori.

5. TEMPORAL SPLIT DESCRIPTION IS INTERNALLY INCONSISTENT. The design states 'Training set (60%): 60,000 patients (~22 months of early data)' and 'Test set (20%): 20,000 patients (~7 months of recent data).' But 22 months + 7 months + 7 months = 36 months, and the dataset spans 3 years (36 months). The arithmetic works. However, with 100,000 patients over 36 months, the monthly admission rate would be approximately 2,777/month. At 60%/20%/20%, validation would be ~7 months and test ~7 months — but these are described as sequential temporal windows. If the split is truly temporal, the split percentages depend on monthly admission volumes, which may not be uniform. The design presents 60/20/20 as equivalent to 22/7/7 months, which requires constant monthly volume — an assumption that is neither stated nor realistic.
all_issues_raised: Vocabulary builder and tokenizer fitted on entire dataset contradicts temporal split; test-period clinical language informs training-time vocabulary construction
Final cross-verification on test set constitutes test-set-based model selection; reported test metrics are not holdout estimates
Assigning single 'most severe complication' label loses multi-complication information and introduces label noise; severity ordering is unspecified and may encode clinical notes language
Complication class prevalence not specified; macro-F1 may be dominated by few-shot rare classes with insufficient training examples for end-to-end transformer fine-tuning
60/20/20 percentage split presented as equivalent to 22/7/7 month temporal windows; assumes constant monthly admission volume that is stated but not established
Preprocessing tokenizer and vocabulary fit on full dataset before splitting leaks test-period clinical language into training preprocessing
Test set used for hyperparameter cross-verification before final model selection, creating optimistic selection bias
Multi-class complication label collapsed to single 'most severe' complication introduces arbitrary label assignments and discards multi-label information
Discharge summaries used as input may encode anticipated post-discharge trajectory, creating a temporal leakage vector for the outcome
Inconsistent preprocessing descriptions between general section 4 (full-dataset fit) and baseline description in section 5 (training-only fit)
Preprocessing fit on entire dataset before splitting leaks test-set vocabulary and scaling statistics into training features
Test set used for cross-verification of hyperparameter configurations during model development, definitively invalidating it as an unbiased estimator
Single-label assignment using severity ranking loses complication co-occurrence information and relies on an unspecified, potentially subjective hierarchy
Macro-averaged F1 on rare complication classes has standard error larger than the minimum clinically meaningful effect size, making it unreliable as a primary metric
